refined_fr.refined_fr: Maintain teleoperation link availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold throughout the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR 'achieve the customer-defined teleoperation link availability' is vague about the temporal and comparative nature of the requirement. The refined version clarifies that availability is a sustained property (not a one-time achievement) measured against a specific threshold over a defined period. This removes ambiguity about what 'achieve' means while remaining solution-neutral—it does not prescribe redundancy, protocol choice, or network architecture. The FR is atomic: it expresses a single obligation (maintain availability above threshold) without embedded 'and'/'or' conditions.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect teleoperation link uptime logs over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (total_uptime / total_period_duration). Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.999). Verify through operational data, test campaigns, or field trials depending on project phase.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant communication pathways with automatic failover logic to maintain continuous link availability when primary pathway degrades or fails.
DP2: Deploy real-time link quality monitoring with predictive degradation detection and proactive rerouting to alternative communication channels before loss of availability occurs.
DP3: Establish hierarchical communication architecture with local autonomous fallback control modes that preserve critical teleoperation functions during link unavailability events.
DP4: Implement adaptive bandwidth and latency management with dynamic protocol selection based on measured link conditions to sustain availability above threshold.
DP5: Combine redundant pathways (DP1) + quality monitoring (DP2) + fallback autonomy (DP3) in an integrated availability assurance system with coordinated state management.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP5: Integrated availability assurance system combining (1) redundant communication pathways with automatic failover, (2) real-time link quality monitoring with predictive rerouting, and (3) hierarchical control architecture with local autonomous fallback modes, coordinated through unified state management and health assessment logic.

potential_sources: Tight coupling between failover logic (DP1) and quality monitoring (DP2) requires well-defined handoff protocols to avoid race conditions or oscillation during marginal link conditions.
Fallback autonomy (DP3) must maintain synchronized state with teleoperation control (DP2) to prevent operator confusion or safety violations during mode transitions.
Unified state management layer introduces a potential single point of logical failure if not properly distributed; requires careful architectural separation of concerns.
Dependency on accurate link quality metrics (DP2) to trigger failover (DP1) and autonomy transitions (DP3); measurement errors propagate across all three sub-DPs.
coupling_check.notes: DP5 is composite but not monolithic. Each sub-DP (redundancy, monitoring, fallback) can be designed independently with clear interfaces. Coupling is managed through explicit state contracts and event-driven coordination rather than direct functional entanglement. This is preferable to single-mechanism approaches (DP1, DP2, DP3 alone) which would fail to meet 0.999 availability in realistic failure scenarios.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Multiple independent communication pathways are available at the worksite (e.g., cellular, licensed radio, satellite, wired backup). Risk: If only one pathway exists, DP1 (redundancy) cannot be realized; recommend early site survey.

ASSUMPTION: Link quality can be measured with sufficient fidelity and latency to enable predictive rerouting (DP2). Risk: Measurement delay or noise may cause false positives/negatives; requires validation testing under representative field conditions.

ASSUMPTION: Fallback autonomous control (DP3) is operationally acceptable and safe per worksite safety plan. Risk: Autonomous behavior may violate site-specific constraints or operator expectations; requires explicit safety case and operator training.

ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is long enough (weeks to months) to capture realistic failure modes and validate 0.999 availability claim. Risk: Short evaluation windows may not expose rare failure modes; recommend minimum 30-day continuous measurement.
ASSUMPTION: Operator and remote equipment clocks are synchronized or clock skew is bounded. Risk: Unsynchronized clocks can cause state divergence during failover; requires NTP or equivalent.

ASSUMPTION: Failover and rerouting latency is acceptable to operator and task requirements. Risk: If latency during transition exceeds operator tolerance, availability may be measured as lost even if link recovers; requires latency budget validation.
RISK: Complexity of integrated system (DP5) increases testing and validation burden; recommend phased integration with DP1 baseline, then DP2, then DP3.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR should be refined to explicitly state: (1) Definition of 'link availability' (is a brief loss of signal during failover counted as unavailability?), (2) Acceptable latency during failover/rerouting, (3) Scope of 'agreed evaluation period' (duration, environmental conditions, failure injection scenarios), (4) Constraints on fallback autonomous behavior (e.g., 'must not exceed speed limit X', 'must maintain line-of-sight to operator'), and (5) Measurement methodology (continuous monitoring, sampling rate, clock synchronization). These refinements will reduce ambiguity in DP validation and acceptance testing.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Centralized module responsible for managing remote vehicle control, communication reliability, and fallback autonomy coordination. Owns the integrated availability assurance system including redundant pathways, link quality monitoring, predictive rerouting, hierarchical control logic, and unified state/health assessment.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface for redundant communication pathways, automatic failover signaling, and real-time link quality telemetry feedback to enable predictive rerouting decisions.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Inbound stream of vehicle state, sensor data, and health metrics required for unified state management and hierarchical control assessment.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Outbound control commands and mode directives to Vehicle, including local autonomous fallback mode activation when communication degrades.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Outbound safety notifications to Safety Services when availability assurance system detects critical link failures or triggers fallback modes.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Teleoperation owns remote control coordination, communication management, and fallback autonomy—all core to DP5. Redundant pathways, link monitoring, predictive rerouting, and hierarchical control with local fallback are natural responsibilities of the teleoperation domain. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert notification and Telemetry consumption cross boundaries as required.

Poor fit. Vehicle module typically owns vehicle dynamics, actuation, and local sensors. DP5's communication redundancy, link quality monitoring, and predictive rerouting logic are not vehicle-internal concerns. Hierarchical control and fallback modes would diffuse across Teleoperation, Safety Services, and Network Link. High coupling and weak cohesion.

Weak fit. Safety Services owns safety policy enforcement and alerts, not communication infrastructure or control coordination. DP5's redundant pathways, link monitoring, and rerouting are operational availability concerns, not safety-specific. Hierarchical control logic would diffuse to Teleoperation. Containment is secondary to Teleoperation.

Very poor fit. NGHE Solution is a top-level system container, not an implementation module. DP5 requires focused ownership of communication and control coordination. Placing DP5 at NGHE level would diffuse responsibility across all child modules and obscure architectural accountability. Overly broad boundary.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the natural owner of DP5 because it is responsible for maintaining reliable remote control of the vehicle across variable communication conditions. Redundant pathways, link quality monitoring, predictive rerouting, and hierarchical fallback modes are all mechanisms to sustain teleoperation capability when primary communication degrades. Unified state management and health assessment are core to coordinating these mechanisms. The module boundary is specific enough to avoid diffusion yet broad enough to contain the full integrated system without fragmentation.
containment_risks: Risk: If Network Link module is independently owned and does not expose sufficient real-time link quality telemetry and failover control interfaces, Teleoperation may lack visibility or authority to implement predictive rerouting. Mitigation: Ensure Network Link interface contract includes detailed link metrics and failover signaling.

Risk: If Vehicle module does not support hierarchical control modes or local autonomous fallback activation, Teleoperation cannot fully realize the fallback logic. Mitigation: Verify Vehicle module accepts mode directives via Tasking interface and implements fallback autonomy handlers.

Risk: If Safety Services is not tightly coupled to Teleoperation's health assessment, critical availability failures may not trigger timely safety alerts. Mitigation: Define clear SafetyAlert contract for availability-critical events (e.g., loss of all redundant pathways).

Risk: Unified state management and health assessment logic may grow complex if Teleoperation also owns other unrelated remote control features. Mitigation: Isolate availability assurance logic into a cohesive sub-component within Teleoperation to maintain clarity.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The FR specifies a measurable acceptance criterion (link availability ≥ 0.999 over agreed evaluation period). The IM boundary (Teleoperation module) owns the integrated availability assurance system and exposes clear interfaces (Network Link for telemetry feedback, Tasking for fallback mode activation, SafetyAlert for critical events) that enable end-to-end verification of availability metrics. Test harnesses can inject link degradation scenarios, monitor failover activation, and measure sustained availability against the threshold.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. The Teleoperation module is unambiguously assigned ownership of redundant pathways, link quality monitoring, predictive rerouting, hierarchical control, and unified state/health assessment. The external interfaces (Network Link inbound, Tasking outbound, SafetyAlert outbound, Telemetry inbound) define the contract boundaries. Responsibility for achieving the FR is traceable to this single module, reducing ambiguity in accountability.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP5 directly addresses the FR. The integrated availability assurance system (redundant pathways + failover, link quality monitoring + predictive rerouting, hierarchical control + local fallback) is a coherent solution to maintaining teleoperation link availability above the customer-defined threshold. The DP is sufficiently detailed to guide implementation (unified state management and health assessment logic are explicit mechanisms) and is not over-constrained to a single technology. The mapping is sound.

diffusion_risks: Risk: Network Link module independence. If Network Link does not expose real-time link quality telemetry (latency, packet loss, signal strength) and failover control signaling with sufficient granularity and latency, Teleoperation cannot implement predictive rerouting effectively. Mitigation: Enforce Network Link interface contract to include detailed link metrics (≥5 Hz update rate) and sub-100ms failover signaling.

Risk: Vehicle module fallback autonomy support. If Vehicle module does not implement hierarchical control modes or does not accept mode directives via Tasking interface (e.g., 'FALLBACK_AUTONOMOUS', 'TELEOPERATED'), Teleoperation cannot activate local autonomous fallback when communication degrades. Mitigation: Verify Vehicle module design includes fallback mode handlers and Tasking interface supports mode activation commands.

Risk: Safety Services coupling. If SafetyAlert interface is loosely coupled or asynchronous, critical availability failures (e.g., loss of all redundant pathways) may not trigger timely safety alerts, violating worksite safety plan requirements. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert contract with synchronous or near-synchronous delivery guarantees for availability-critical events; specify alert payload to include failure mode and fallback status.

Risk: Unified state management complexity. If Teleoperation module accumulates unrelated remote control features (e.g., gesture recognition, multi-user arbitration, sensor fusion), the availability assurance logic may become entangled and difficult to test/verify. Mitigation: Isolate availability assurance logic (redundancy coordination, link monitoring, predictive rerouting, hierarchical control) into a cohesive sub-component or internal module within Teleoperation; maintain clear separation of concerns.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Excellent containment fit (0.92 score). Teleoperation is the natural owner of DP5 because remote control coordination, communication reliability, and fallback autonomy are core to its domain. Redundant pathways, link quality monitoring, predictive rerouting, and hierarchical control are mechanisms to sustain teleoperation capability under degraded conditions. Unified state management and health assessment are essential to coordinating these mechanisms. Minimal diffusion: only SafetyAlert notification (outbound) and Telemetry consumption (inbound) cross module boundaries as required by the architecture. No evidence of scattered responsibilities or duplicated logic across Vehicle, Safety Services, or NGHE Solution. The module boundary is specific enough to avoid diffusion yet broad enough to contain the full integrated system without fragmentation.

recommended_revisions: Formalize Network Link interface contract: Specify minimum telemetry update frequency (≥5 Hz), link quality metrics (latency, packet loss, signal strength, jitter), and failover signaling latency (target <100 ms). Document how Teleoperation will consume these metrics for predictive rerouting decisions.

Verify Vehicle module fallback autonomy design: Confirm that Vehicle module accepts hierarchical control mode directives via Tasking interface and implements local autonomous fallback handlers. Document the mode activation protocol and fallback behavior (e.g., hover, return-to-home, safe descent).

Define SafetyAlert contract for availability-critical events: Specify which availability assurance failures trigger SafetyAlert (e.g., loss of all redundant pathways, repeated failover cycles, link quality below safety threshold). Ensure alert delivery is synchronous or near-synchronous and includes failure mode and fallback status.

Isolate availability assurance sub-component: Within Teleoperation, create a cohesive internal module (e.g., 'AvailabilityAssurance' or 'LinkReliabilityManager') that encapsulates redundancy coordination, link monitoring, predictive rerouting, and hierarchical control logic. This improves testability, maintainability, and clarity of responsibility.

Define acceptance test plan: Specify test scenarios that inject link degradation (latency spikes, packet loss, pathway failures), verify failover activation, measure sustained availability, and confirm SafetyAlert triggering. Ensure test coverage maps to the 0.999 availability criterion over the agreed evaluation period.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, FR-DP, DP-IM) pass with high confidence. The FR is measurable (link availability ≥ 0.999), the DP is coherent and sufficiently detailed (integrated availability assurance system with redundancy, monitoring, predictive rerouting, hierarchical control), and the IM (Teleoperation module) is the correct owner with clear interface contracts. However, the recommended_revisions and diffusion_risks reveal critical dependencies and internal complexity that must be decomposed to ensure implementability and verifiability. Specifically: (1) Network Link interface contract must be formalized to guarantee telemetry granularity and failover signaling latency; (2) Vehicle module fallback autonomy support must be verified as a prerequisite; (3) SafetyAlert contract must be defined for availability-critical events; (4) Unified state management complexity suggests internal decomposition of availability assurance logic into a cohesive sub-component. These are not design flaws but rather signals that the next level of functional decomposition is necessary to operationalize DP5 and mitigate implementation risks. Given high safety criticality, decomposition is prudent to expose and resolve dependencies early.
next_level_sub_frs: FR5.1: Monitor and assess real-time link quality (latency, packet loss, signal strength, jitter) from all redundant pathways at ≥5 Hz update frequency to enable predictive rerouting decisions.
FR5.2: Detect link degradation and failover triggers (e.g., latency threshold breach, packet loss spike, pathway unavailability) and activate failover to alternate pathway within <100 ms.
FR5.3: Predict link degradation trends and proactively reroute teleoperation traffic to healthier pathways before critical threshold breach to maintain continuous availability.

FR5.4: Coordinate hierarchical control modes (teleoperated vs. local autonomous fallback) and activate Vehicle fallback autonomy (e.g., hover, return-to-home) when all redundant pathways are unavailable or degraded below safety threshold.
FR5.5: Maintain unified state and health assessment of all redundant pathways, failover status, and control mode to enable consistent decision-making and SafetyAlert triggering.

FR5.6: Trigger SafetyAlert notifications to Safety Services when availability assurance fails (e.g., loss of all redundant pathways, repeated failover cycles, link quality below safety threshold) with synchronous or near-synchronous delivery and failure mode context.

revise.why: DP5 is sound at the current level but requires explicit sub-DP articulation to address the four key mechanisms: (1) Link Quality Monitoring DP (real-time telemetry consumption and metric aggregation), (2) Failover Detection & Activation DP (threshold-based trigger logic and pathway switching), (3) Predictive Rerouting DP (trend analysis and proactive traffic steering), (4) Hierarchical Control Coordination DP (mode state machine and Vehicle fallback activation), (5) Unified State Management DP (centralized health assessment and consistency), (6) SafetyAlert Signaling DP (event filtering and synchronous notification). These sub-DPs will clarify the internal architecture of Teleoperation and resolve the 'unified state management complexity' risk by making the separation of concerns explicit.

assumptions: Network Link module will expose real-time link quality telemetry (latency, packet loss, signal strength, jitter) at ≥5 Hz update frequency via a well-defined interface contract.
Network Link module will support failover signaling with latency <100 ms to enable rapid pathway switching.
Vehicle module implements hierarchical control modes and accepts mode directives (e.g., 'FALLBACK_AUTONOMOUS', 'TELEOPERATED') via Tasking interface.
Vehicle module has local autonomous fallback handlers (e.g., hover, return-to-home, safe descent) that can be activated when teleoperation link is unavailable.
SafetyAlert interface supports synchronous or near-synchronous delivery of availability-critical events with sufficient payload detail (failure mode, fallback status).
Teleoperation module will isolate availability assurance logic into a cohesive internal sub-component to avoid entanglement with unrelated remote control features.
Evaluation period for 0.999 availability criterion is defined and agreed with customer (e.g., 30 days, 90 days).
Link quality thresholds (latency, packet loss, signal strength) that trigger failover or fallback activation are defined in safety/operational requirements.
open_risks: Network Link interface contract may not be formalized with sufficient detail (telemetry frequency, metric granularity, failover signaling latency). Mitigation: Enforce interface specification review and integration testing before Teleoperation implementation begins.

Vehicle module fallback autonomy support may be incomplete or missing. Mitigation: Verify Vehicle module design early; if fallback modes are not implemented, escalate to Vehicle team or revise FR5.4 scope.
SafetyAlert coupling may be loose or asynchronous, delaying critical availability failure notifications. Mitigation: Define SafetyAlert contract with delivery guarantees and test end-to-end latency.

Unified state management in Teleoperation may accumulate unrelated features (gesture recognition, multi-user arbitration, sensor fusion), increasing complexity and test burden. Mitigation: Enforce internal module boundaries and code review discipline; isolate availability assurance sub-component.

Predictive rerouting logic may require machine learning or statistical models that are difficult to verify and validate in safety-critical context. Mitigation: Define predictive rerouting algorithm (e.g., exponential moving average, threshold-based heuristic) and validation approach early.

Failover latency (<100 ms) may be difficult to achieve if Network Link or Vehicle modules have higher inherent latencies. Mitigation: Conduct latency budget analysis across all modules; if target is unachievable, revise FR5.2 or escalate to system-level requirements.
evidence_needed: Network Link interface specification: Telemetry update frequency, link quality metrics (latency, packet loss, signal strength, jitter), failover signaling protocol, and latency guarantees.
Vehicle module design review: Confirmation of hierarchical control mode support, fallback autonomy handlers, and Tasking interface protocol for mode activation.
SafetyAlert interface contract: Event types, delivery mechanism (synchronous vs. asynchronous), payload schema, and latency requirements for availability-critical events.

Teleoperation internal architecture design: Sub-component decomposition (Link Quality Monitor, Failover Detector, Predictive Router, Hierarchical Control Coordinator, Unified State Manager, SafetyAlert Signaler) with clear separation of concerns.

Acceptance test plan: Test scenarios for link degradation injection, failover activation, sustained availability measurement, and SafetyAlert triggering. Test coverage mapping to 0.999 availability criterion.
Latency budget analysis: End-to-end latency breakdown across Network Link, Teleoperation, Vehicle, and SafetyAlert modules. Verification that failover latency target (<100 ms) is achievable.
Predictive rerouting algorithm specification: Mathematical model, input metrics, decision logic, and validation approach (e.g., simulation, field testing).

Safety case evidence: Traceability from FR5 (and sub-FRs) to safety requirements, hazard analysis, and mitigation strategies. Confirmation that availability assurance design addresses identified hazards.
